name: Moon
description: Earth's companion, a quarter of Earth's diameter — proportionally the largest moon of any planet. Its distance is famously underestimated: all seven other planets would fit in the gap.
facts: Light from the Moon reaches Earth in 1.3 seconds.
The Moon drifts 3.8 cm farther from Earth every year.
It is tidally locked: the same face always points at Earth.

name: Phobos
description: Mars's larger moon orbits so close and so fast that it rises in the west and sets in the east — twice a day.
facts: Phobos is spiralling inward and will crash into Mars or shatter into a ring within ~50 million years.

name: Deimos
description: Mars's smaller, outer moon — a lumpy rock just 12 km across, probably a captured asteroid.
facts: From Mars's surface, Deimos looks like a bright star rather than a moon.

name: Io
description: The most volcanically active world in the Solar System, kneaded by Jupiter's tides. Hundreds of volcanoes erupt sulfur across its yellow surface.
facts: Io's volcanic plumes can rise 500 km into space.

name: Europa
description: An ice-covered moon hiding a global ocean with more liquid water than all of Earth's oceans combined — a prime candidate in the search for life.
facts: The cracked 'chaos terrain' on its surface suggests the ice shell shifts over the ocean below.

name: Ganymede
description: The largest moon in the Solar System — bigger than the planet Mercury — and the only moon with its own magnetic field.
facts: Ganymede also likely hides a deep subsurface ocean beneath its icy crust.

name: Callisto
description: The most heavily cratered object known — a fossil surface unchanged for 4 billion years, orbiting far outside Jupiter's radiation belts.
facts: Its distance from Jupiter's radiation makes Callisto a favoured site for hypothetical crewed bases.

name: Enceladus
description: A small, brilliantly white moon that shoots geysers of salty water from its south pole — direct samples of a subsurface ocean, delivered to space.
facts: Enceladus reflects nearly 100% of the sunlight that hits it, making it the most reflective body in the Solar System.
Its geysers feed Saturn's E ring.

name: Titan
description: Saturn's giant moon, the only moon with a thick atmosphere — and the only other world with rain, rivers, lakes and seas. There, the liquid is methane at −179 °C.
facts: Titan's atmosphere is denser than Earth's.
The Huygens probe landed here in 2005 — the most distant landing ever made.

name: Rhea
description: Saturn's second-largest moon, an ancient ball of dirty water ice, heavily cratered and quiet.

name: Iapetus
description: The two-faced moon: one hemisphere is coal-dark, the other snow-bright. A mysterious equatorial ridge makes it look like a walnut.
facts: Its bright side is about ten times more reflective than its dark side.

name: Miranda
description: A patchwork moon of cliffs and canyons that looks reassembled from broken pieces. Its cliff Verona Rupes is up to 20 km tall — the highest known.
facts: Jumping off Verona Rupes, you would fall for about 12 minutes.

name: Titania
description: The largest moon of Uranus, scarred by enormous canyons from an era when its interior froze and expanded.

name: Oberon
description: The outermost large moon of Uranus, old and cratered, named — like all Uranian moons — after a Shakespeare character.

name: Triton
description: Neptune's big moon orbits backwards — the only large moon that does — a telltale sign it was captured from the Kuiper belt. Nitrogen geysers erupt from its frozen surface.
facts: Triton's retrograde orbit is slowly decaying; in a few billion years Neptune's gravity will tear it apart.
At −235 °C it is one of the coldest places known.

name: Charon
description: Pluto's giant companion — half Pluto's diameter, so large that the pair form a true double dwarf-planet system.
facts: Pluto and Charon always show each other the same face: both are tidally locked.
